coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free carbon dots@MgO nanocomposite as an efficient sensor for on-site detection and degradation of paraoxon toxins

Cai, Meijie
Feng, Xiangling
Lu, Siyu
Lin, Song
Tian, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Serial nanocomposites consisting of blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) and MgO nanoparticles were facilely prepared. Then on-site detection and degradation of ethyl-paraoxon were achieved and evaluated. It was revealed that ethyl-paraoxon was firstly decomposed into p-nitrophenol by MgO. Then, p-nitrophenol could significantly quench the fluorescence of decorated B-CDs due to an inner filter effect (IFE). The 0.05B-CDs@MgO showed excellent comprehensive performance among the serial xB-CDs@MgO samples due to its relatively high degradation ability towards ethyl-paraoxon and potent initial fluorescence emission intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm between pH 4 and 10. Finally, based on the IFE, on-site paper sensors via color recognition app on a smartphone were explored compared with a conventional fluorescence spectrometer. Both the quantitative fluorescence intensities and G/B values showed the multi-piecewise linear correlations to ethyl-paraoxon concentration up to 3000 μg/mL in river water with a limit of detection to 0.06 μg/mL and 3.48 μg/mL as well as high recovery rates between 99%-102% and 95%–116%, respectively. The in vitro cytotoxicity and phytotoxicity assessment of the 0.05B-CDs@MgO revealed dose-dependent effects. Such portable and integrated fluorescent B-CDs@MgO nanocomposites on paper arrays show excellent potential in shortening the detection time and reducing the detection cost as a new strategy for the on-site quantitative detection and degradation of ethyl-paraoxon in actual river water.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free carbon dots@MgO nanocomposite as an efficient sensor for on-site detection and degradation of paraoxon toxins

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serial nanocomposites consisting of blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) and MgO nanoparticles were facilely prepared. Then on-site detection and degradation of ethyl-paraoxon were achieved and evaluated. It was revealed that ethyl-paraoxon was firstly decomposed into p-nitrophenol by MgO. Then, p-nitrophenol could significantly quench the fluorescence of decorated B-CDs due to an inner filter effect (IFE). The 0.05B-CDs@MgO showed excellent comprehensive performance among the serial xB-CDs@MgO samples due to its relatively high degradation ability towards ethyl-paraoxon and potent initial fluorescence emission intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm between pH 4 and 10. Finally, based on the IFE, on-site paper sensors via color recognition app on a smartphone were explored compared with a conventional fluorescence spectrometer. Both the quantitative fluorescence intensities and G/B values showed the multi-piecewise linear correlations to ethyl-paraoxon concentration up to 3000 μg/mL in river water with a limit of detection to 0.06 μg/mL and 3.48 μg/mL as well as high recovery rates between 99%-102% and 95%–116%, respectively. The in vitro cytotoxicity and phytotoxicity assessment of the 0.05B-CDs@MgO revealed dose-dependent effects. Such portable and integrated fluorescent B-CDs@MgO nanocomposites on paper arrays show excellent potential in shortening the detection time and reducing the detection cost as a new strategy for the on-site quantitative detection and degradation of ethyl-paraoxon in actual river water.

Serial integrated xB-CDs@MgO nanocomposites were constructed and optimized as enzyme-free sensors for on-site detection and effective degradation of ethyl-paraoxon toxins simultaneously. The degradation capacity of ethyl-paraoxon was up to 50.89 mg/g. Furthermore, two detection setups, i.e., a conventional fluorescence method and a visual determination by a smartphone, were explored based on the inner filter effect (IFE). Both the quantitative fluorescence intensities and G/B values revealed the piecewise linear correlation to ethyl-paraoxon concentration up to 3000 μg/mL in river water with LOD of 0.06 μg/mL and 3.48 μg/mL, respectively. Moreover, both methods showed high recovery rates in river water between 99%–102% and 95%–116%.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus compounds, as typical pesticides, are widely used in agricultural [1–5] and military fields [3,6]. Since they can quickly enter the human body through inhalation or skin contact [7], causing significant risks such as respiratory system disorder, fibrillation, paralysis, and even death [8,9]. Both the natural environment [2,3] and multitudinous human beings [10–12] worldwide suffer yearly paraoxon-related exposure due to their residual toxicity [13,14]. Therefore, on-site detection and detoxification of paraoxon in the environment for effective environmental remediation are essential.

Magnesium oxide (MgO) is non-toxic, environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and highly catalytic inorganic metal oxide [15–18]. MgO nanoparticles have enormous destructive adsorption properties and high adsorption efficiency due to their relatively small size and high specific surface area, so it is widely used in the adsorption and degradation of phosphorus pesticides and simulated chemical warfare agents [16,19–23]. The phosphorus atom bears a positive charge in the molecule, which endows high electrophilicity to react with nucleophilic substances easily [18,22,24,25]. This allows phosphate groups to form stable complexes with magnesium oxide through electrostatic adsorption [16,25]. Generally, the degradation and catalytic activities of MgO are inversely proportional to its particle size [19,20,26], which was also confirmed by our previous research [27]. Paraoxon has a strong affinity with the MgO surface [15]. The MgO surface contains superoxide free radicals (•O2 −) and •OH, which may cause paraoxon decomposition into p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and other byproducts [15,28,29].

Conventional paraoxon detection methods include gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, the combination of chromatography and mass spectrometry, and enzyme reagents [1,30–35]. However, these methods are expensive or time-consuming, suffering from storage instability or complicated instrumental assistance procedures [31–36]. These challenges render the inconvenience for routine paraoxon monitoring. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a facile and portable platform for the rapid on-site detection and detoxification of paraoxon.

Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the novel carbon-based nanomaterials with great potential in sensing and imaging applications due to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence characteristics, good photostability, easy synthesis, and versatile surface modification [31–33,35,37–41]. In recent years, sensor strategy of CDs based on the internal filtering effect (IFE) has gradually prospered [42–47], which is highly sensitive, flexible, and simple [45,48]. Mondal et al. [45] prepared pyridine-rich CDs by l-malic acid and urea. Based on IFE effect, the pyridine-rich CDs were sensitive and selective for detecting the nerve agent analog diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP). The detection range for DCP was 3–15 mM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 84 μM. Zhong et al. [46] used citric acid and formamide to prepare red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs). Via IFE, R-CDs could be used to detect mitoxantrone (MITX), an anticancer drug in human serum with a detection range of 0.096–30 μM and a LOD of 0.032 μM. Luo et al. [31] presented a two-step method for routine methyl paraoxon (MP) detection in actual samples. At first, the prepared cerium oxide (CeO2) was used to decompose MP into p-NP and phosphate groups. Then, p-NP could reduce the fluorescence intensity of boron nitrogen-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) by IFE. The linear range of MP was 0.015–1.5 mM.

Based on the highly efficient degradation of MgO and the rapid detection of carbon dots towards paraoxon, we combined blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs) with as-prepared MgO nanoparticles to obtain serial xB-CDs@MgO (x = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1) nanocomposites with different composition ratios. Via physical and chemical analysis, it was found that the optimum 0.05B-CDs@MgO showed excellent comprehensive performance among the serial samples. MgO nanoparticles could produce •O2 − and •OH sites, effectively decomposing ethyl-paraoxon into p-NP and other phosphoric groups [27]. Via IFE, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs could be reduced by p-NP [31]. The degradation amount of ethyl-paraoxon was up to 50.89 mg/g. The preliminary study revealed that the fluorescence intensity showed a perfectly piecewise linear relationship versus ethyl-paraoxon concentration (0–100 μg/mL, R2 = 0.99), which confirmed the hypothesis and prompted us to explore the situation further for detecting ethyl-paraoxon in actual river samples. In the actual river sample, the detection range of ethyl-paraoxon was 0–3000 μg/mL, and LOD was 0.06 μg/mL with a recovery rate of 99%–102% via fluorescence spectra. In the meantime, the visual color of B-CDs changed dramatically from cyan-blue to yellow-green.

Furthermore, based on this phenomenon, a facile and portable paper-based array sensor was adapted for on-site batch detection through a smartphone's color recognition APP (Lan Se@App store). The green/blue channel ratio (G/B) values were captured to determine the quantitative concentration of ethyl-paraoxon according to the predetermined multi-stage standard curves. The LOD was 3.48 μg/mL, and the recovery rate was 95%–116%. Such an enzyme-free nanocomposite sensor may provide a new idea for developing a reliable, convenient, effective, and portable platform for ethyl-paraoxon toxin on-site detection and detoxification.

The citric acid (AR), Ethylenediamine (AR), and Magnesium methoxide (Mg(OCH3)2;7-8 wt%) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Toluene (AR) and absolute ethanol (AR) were provided by Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Ethyl-paraoxon (AR) was supplied by Toronto Research Chemicals (Canada). Deionized (DI) water (18.0 MΩ cm) was produced in our laboratory. The actual river water was acquired in the Haihe River in August 2022 (Tianjin, China). Human skin fibroblasts (HSF) cells were purchased from Saibaikang Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

B-CDs were prepared in a vacuum drying oven (LC-213, Shanghai Espec Environmental Equipment Co., Ltd.). MgO was prepared in a vacuum/atmosphere tube furnace (SK-G05143, Tianjin Zhonghuan Experimental Furnace Co., Ltd.). HRTEM images and element mappings were taken on a 300 kV Talos F200XG2 AEMC (FEI, USA) electron microscope. SEM images were taken on TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech. FT-IR spectra were recorded by a Thermo FTIR spectrophotometer. The emission spectra were performed at a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FS5, UK). The XRD analysis was obtained on Ultima IV (Rikuta, Japan). The XPS analysis was performed at Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA). The specific surface area and porosity of the sample were obtained by using an automatic specific surface area and porosity analyzer (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA). The Zeta potential of samples was recorded on Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZS, Malvern Panalytical, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of B-CDs and MgO nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized using the previous method [49]. Generally, 2.1 g of citric acid and 2.68 mL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 60 mL of DI water, and then the solution was treated at 200 °C for 5 h. The raw product was dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was collected and frozen dried to obtain a powder.

MgO was prepared based on the previous work [27]. 50 mL Mg(OCH3)2 in methanol and 131 mL toluene were thoroughly mixed under a high-purity nitrogen atmosphere. Then, it was titrated with 1.75 mL of deionized water dropwise. Mg(OH)2 gel was obtained after overnight stirring (25 °C) and reacted in an autoclave at 265 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, it was ventilated, filtered, and washed with methanol several times. The aerogel Mg(OH)2 was obtained after drying at 120 °C for 2 h. Then the above Mg(OH)2 was transferred into the tubular furnace and calcined under N2. The peak calcination temperature was set at 600 °C for 2 h (the heating rate was 1 °C/min) to obtain the as-prepared MgO nanoparticles.

Some modifications were made to obtain nanocomposite of B-CDs@MgO as in previous reports [17,50]. 0.1 g MgO and the different charge amounts of B-CDs (0.01 g, 0.02 g, 0.05 g, 0.1 g) were ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL of absolute ethanol and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, the suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to leave yellow sediment of xB-CDs@MgO (x = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1). The above xB-CDs@MgO was washed for several cycles with ethanol until the supernatant was free from the fluorescence signal. Finally, the products were dried and stored in sealed bags for further investigation.

xB-CDs@MgO and MgO (2 mg) were added into 1 mL of ethyl-paraoxon aqueous solution (200 μg/mL). The maximum absorption peak of 268 nm at different time intervals (0, 15, 30, and 60 min) was measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The percentage of ethyl-paraoxon degradation was acquired according to Eq. (1), and the degradation amounts of ethyl-paraoxon were calculated according to Eq. (2).
(1)

Abs0 is the absorbance value of ethyl-paraoxon at t = 0, Abst is the absorbance value of ethyl-paraoxon at time t, qt (mg/g) is the adsorption and degradation amount of ethyl-paraoxon at time t, m0 is the initial mass of ethyl-paraoxon in solution (m0 = 200 μg), m is the mass of MgO (m = 2 mg).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ethyl-paraoxon in deionized water
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mL of ethyl-paraoxon aqueous solution was prepared with different concentrations, and 2 mg of 0.05B-CDs@MgO was added to the above solution. After 30 min of incubation, the fluorescence intensity of each sample was tested at 360 nm excitation condition. The fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of ethyl-paraoxon was F0 and F, respectively. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F vs. the concentration of ethyl-paraoxon was analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ethyl-paraoxon in river water by fluorescence spectrometer
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, the actual river water was taken from a local river (Haihe River, Tianjin). The crude water sample was centrifuged at 12000 rpm and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the insoluble substances. Then, the different concentrations of ethyl-paraoxon were added to the pretreated river water (1 mL), and 3 mg of 0.05B-CDs@MgO was added to the above solution. The fluorescence intensity of each sample was measured at 360 nm excitation condition after 30 min. The fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of ethyl-paraoxon was F0 and F, respectively. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F vs. ethyl-paraoxon concentration was analyzed.

The blank paper-based array was manufactured by a wax jet printer (ColorQube) to obtain a series of circular filter paper (ca. 8 mm) surrounded by the sprayed hydrophobic paraffin. Then, 10 μL of 0.05B-CDs@MgO (3 mg/mL) was added dropwise to the circular filter paper area, dried at 80 °C in a vacuum drying oven, and stored for further tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ethyl-paraoxon in river water based on a paper-based array
ce:section.ce:para: Smartphone and paper-based array sensors were used to analyze ethyl-paraoxon in river water. The process was similar to the fluorescence spectrometer, and the data was recorded and analyzed by the smartphone app (Lan Se@App Store).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro phytotoxicity and cytotoxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the ecological toxicity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO, the phytotoxicity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO to mung beans (Vigna radiata) was evaluated [51]. After being disinfected with NaClO (1.2%), the mung beans were thoroughly washed with DI water and soaked in 10, 100, and 500 μg/mL of 0.05B-CDs@MgO, respectively, by adding enough water when necessary. DI water treatment was used as the blank control. After 1, 3, and 7 days, the germination rate and the rhizome length were measured and evaluated.

The cytotoxicity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO was evaluated by using the human dermal fibroblasts (HSF) cells. HSF cells (1 × 105 cells/mL) were added to the 96 wells plate of DMEM with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin cultured in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. HSF cells were treated with 0.05B-CDs@MgO with the concentration of 1, 10, 100, 500, 1000 μg/mL for 24 h. Then, MTT assay was added to measure the optical density of each well to calculate the cell viability according to the following Eq. (3).
(3)

Fig. 1 demonstrates the synthetic routes of serial xB-CDs@MgO samples and the strategy for on-site detecting and degrading ethyl-paraoxon toxins based on the 0.05B-CDs@MgO. Fig. 1(A) and (B) briefly described the preparation process of B-CDs and nano-MgO, respectively. Fig. 1 (C) showed the preparation of xB-CDs@MgO nanocompoites and their detection and degradation mechanisms towards ethyl-paraoxon. When incubated with MgO, ethyl-paraoxon was firstly decomposed into p-NP, which could decrease B-CDs' fluorescence intensity due to IFE. Furthermore, a paper-based array sensor based on 0.05B-CDs@MgO was developed, as shown in Fig. 1 (D). Then, a drop of ethyl-paraoxon with a gradient concentration in the river samples was added to the paper sensor. Finally, a smartphone was utilized to capture the fluorescence images under UV 365 nm irradiation, and a color recognition APP was used to determine the recorded RGB value of each test sample. Thus, by referring to the predetermined standard curves of ethyl-paraoxon, the on-site detection and degradation of serial ethyl-paraoxon solutions could be achieved within 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structures of serial xB-CDs@MgO
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and SEM tests were carried out to observe the morphology and structures of xB-CDs@MgO. As previously reported, the as-prepared B-CDs were well dispersed with particle sizes of 2.5 ± 1.5 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.17 nm [49]. The as-prepared MgO nanoparticles had a mesoporous structure (Fig. 2 A and B). The result was also confirmed by selective area electron diffraction (SAED) (Fig. 2C), consistent with the previous reports [27,52]. Similar results were observed in xB-CDs@MgO samples. Both the B-CDs and MgO coexist in all the xB-CDs@MgO nanocomposites (Fig. 2E, H, 2K, 2 N). The TEM of xB-CDs@MgO (Fig. 2D, G, 2J, 2 M) showed that different ratios of B-CDs had no apparent effect on the morphology of xB-CDs@MgO as they all showed spherical agglomeration under SEM observation (Fig. S1). Two kinds of spherical crystals in HRTEM of xB-CDs@MgO were detected with the measured lattice spacings of 0.28 nm and 0.17 nm, corresponding to MgO and B-CDs respectively (Fig. 2E, H, 2K, 2 N). The results were also confirmed by selected electron diffraction (SEAD) patterns as in Fig. 2F, I, 2 L, and 2O. The elemental mappings of serial xB-CDs@MgO contained all elements of B-CDs and MgO (C, N, O, and Mg) (Fig. S2). Moreover, there was no separation between B-CDs and MgO after cycled ultrasonic and washing treatment, which proved that the combination between B-CDs and MgO is relatively tight. These results could preliminarily prove that the nanocomposite of serial xB-CDs@MgO had been obtained successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of physical and chemical evaluation of xB-CDs@MgO
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, statistical particle sizes, BET, FT-IR, XRD, XPS, and zeta potential of xB-CDs@MgO were performed. Compared with pure MgO, the particle sizes of xB-CDs@MgO increased significantly. The average particle size increased from 8.2 ± 1.3 nm to 11.3 ± 2.8 nm, 11.4 ± 2.1 nm, 11.8 ± 1.6 nm, and 11.0 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. 3 A) of xB-CDs@MgO (x = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1), respectively, due to the incorporation of B-CDs onto MgO. At the same time, the varied contents of B-CDs show no apparent effects on the size dimension of xB-CDs@MgO.

The specific surface area and pore volume of MgO and xB-CDs@MgO were analyzed by BET. From the pore size distribution (Fig. 3B), the major micropores and mesopores of xB-CDs@MgO were less than 20 nm, and the pore diameter was almost the same as pristine MgO. Fig. 3C showed that the specific surface area of MgO and xB-CDs@MgO (x = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1) were 238.8 m2/g, 241.8 m2/g, 191.8 m2/g, 218.6 m2/g and 207.7 m2/g, respectively (Table S1). Fig. 3D showed that B-CDs had a negative zeta potential of −2.24 mV, and pure MgO had a high positive zeta potential of 45.5 mV. However, The zeta potential of 0.05B-CDs@MgO was 35.7 mV, which compromised the surface charge of individual B-CDs and MgO.

Fig. 3E shows the FT-IR spectra of B-CDs, MgO, and xB-CDs@MgO. The wide absorption bands between 3700 cm−1 to 3080 cm−1 were caused by the shrinkage of hydroxyl groups. With the increasing combination of B-CDs, the hydroxyl absorption of xB-CDs@MgO was enhanced. The absorption peaks of xB-CDs@MgO at 1662 cm−1 and 937 cm−1 were assigned as CO stretching and –COOH in-plane bending, respectively. In addition, the absorption bands of xB-CDs@MgO, i.e., 1593 cm−1 to 1362 cm−1, 861 cm−1, and 419 cm−1 were consistent with pure MgO. The absorption peak of 1593 cm−1 to 1362 cm−1 might be caused by the formation of carbonate [53,54]. The absorption peaks at 861 cm−1 and 419 cm−1 might be caused by the vibration of MgO [20,55].

Via X-ray diffraction analysis, it was found that the diffraction peaks of xB-CDs@MgO were detected corresponding to the crystal planes (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) of MgO [52,53] (Fig. 3F). In addition, a broad diffraction peak appeared within 15–30° consistent with pristine B-CDs (inset in Fig. 3F). XPS spectra showed that in the binding energy range of 0–1300 eV, all the prominent characteristic peaks of xB-CDs@MgO (C, O, N, and Mg) had been confirmed (Fig. S3). Compared with the N1s peak of MgO, the peak at ca. 405 eV was enhanced in xB-CDs@MgO, which might be related to the C–N–H from B-CDs.

The fluorescence property was a significant merit of xB-CDs@MgO as the hypothesis basis for the detection application. High fluorescent candidate materials should have strong anti-background interference ability and higher detection precision [56,57], so screening optimum fluorescent conditions of xB-CDs@MgO was crucial. Fig. 4 showed that xB-CDs@MgO had strong blue fluorescence emission around 400–500 nm with excitation wavelength (Ex.) of 310–380 nm. The most substantial emission peak appeared at 443 nm with an Ex. of 360 nm, which displayed similar fluorescence performance with pristine B-CDs [49]. The varied emission peak (310–380 nm) of xB-CDs@MgO did not show excitation dependence without a red shift to a higher wavelength. The main reason might be the single near-band edge transition during the excitation of xB-CDs@MgO [49,58]. When B-CDs were excited at different wavelengths, the surface wells of xB-CDs@MgO were effectively passivated by functional groups. For each xB-CDs@MgO sample (Fig. 4A–D), the highest fluorescence intensity was achieved at Ex. of 360 nm. Fig. 4E showed the compared fluorescence emission intensity of xB-CDs@MgO at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO was more potent than others, indicating the optimum combination of B-CDs and MgO under this ratio. As shown in Fig. 4F, compared with B-CDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of 0.05B-CDs@MgO and 0.1B-CDs@MgO were significantly enhanced, while the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs, 0.01B-CDs@MgO, and 0.02B-CDs@MgO were almost identical (Fig. S4). The reason might be that the Mg element can enhance the fluorescence property. The doping of MgO might change the electronic structures of B-CDs by adjusting the HOMO-LUMO energy gap or band gap of B-CDs [59–62]. The content of B-CDs in 0.05B-CDs@MgO and 0.1B-CDs@MgO was relatively higher, leading to more exposure to Mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of xB-CDs@MgO towards ethyl-paraoxon degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have revealed the degradation mechanism of ethyl-paraoxon by MgO. Specifically, the oxygen dissolved in the solution generated superoxide radical anion •O2 − through the molecular transition and electronic rearrangement process on the MgO surface to achieve effective ethyl-paraoxon degradation (Fig. S5) [27,63,64]. Moreover, the color of MgO powders changed from white to yellowish because of the degraded byproduct of p-nitrophenol adsorption [27]. Fig. 5 A shows the elemental mapping of 0.05B-CDs@MgO after the degradation of ethyl-paraoxon. The P element was found to distribute evenly over the 0.05B-CDs@MgO, which indicated ethyl-paraoxon could be adsorbed and enriched onto the surface of 0.05B-CDs@MgO. The degradation ability of ethyl-paraoxon by xB-CDs@MgO was evaluated and illustrated inFig. 5B and C, and S6. The xB-CDs@MgO show different abilities towards ethyl-paraoxon degradation. The trends of 0.01B-CDs@MgO and 0.05B-CDs@MgO were similar and relatively higher than that of 0.02B-CDs@MgO and 0.1B-CDs@MgO, which could be due to the larger specific surface areas (Table S1). The degradation rate reached a plateau at ca. 30 min. The degradation amount qt of 0.01B-CDs@MgO and 0.05B-CDs@MgO was up to 52.17 mg/g and 50.89 mg/g within 60 min, respectively. Overall, the degradation amount qt of xB-CDs@MgO was less than pristine MgO, possibly because of the coating of B-CDs onto MgO nanoparticles to hinder some reaction sites with ethyl-paraoxon. However, the maximum degradation capacity of ethyl-paraoxon by xB-CDs@MgO was still superior to some previous studies (Table S2).

The FTIR spectra before and after the degradation of ethyl-paraoxon by 0.05B-CDs@MgO were illustrated in Fig. 5D. It was found that 0.05B-CDs@MgO was changed obviously after interaction with ethyl-paraoxon. The absorption peak at 3700 cm−1 was obviously enhanced, which might be derived from more gaseous H2O vibration absorption. The absorption peak increased at 1654 cm−1 and 1416 cm−1 from B-CDs. After the degradation reaction, surface morphology transformed into flower-like structures (Fig. S7). The absorption peaks at 1282 cm−1 and 563 cm−1 might be caused by PO shrinkage of phosphate ester and asymmetric angle change of PO4, respectively. In order to further verify the degradation of ethyl-paraoxon, the XPS spectra before and after the adsorption of ethyl-paraoxon by 0.05B-CDs@MgO were shown in Fig. 5E and S8. In the binding energy range of 0–1400 eV, all the prominent characteristic peaks of C, O, N, and Mg appeared (Fig. S8A), and the peak of N1s (400 eV) appeared to be enhanced (Fig. S8B). A new P2p peak (ca. 134 eV) was detected (Fig. 5E), indicating that ethyl-paraoxon was successfully adsorbed and degraded by 0.05B-CDs@MgO. Via comprehensive evaluation performance of different xB-CDs@MgO, 0.05B-CDs@MgO was superior to other xB-CDs@MgO samples regarding fluorescence detection and degradation performance. Thus, 0.05B-CDs@MgO was selected as a candidate in the following study.

The pH is a crucial factor affecting the detection and degradation ability [65]. Thus, we explored the stability and degradation performance of 0.05B-CDs@MgO under different pH. As shown in Fig. S9A, there were no obvious state changes between 0.05B-CDs@MgO and the commercial MgO, indicating that 0.05B-CDs@MgO were stale in aqueous solutions between the pH of 4–10. However, when the pH was decreased to 1, both were utterly dissolved. Moreover, there was no apparent difference in the degradation amounts and rates of ethyl-paraoxon by 0.05B-CDs@MgO between the pH 4 to 10 (Figs. S9B and S9C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ethyl-paraoxon in DI water and actual river water
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7A, similar trends could be observed in DI water. The fluorescence intensity showed concentration dependence of ethyl-paraoxon (0–3000 μg/mL). The fluorescence intensity decreased by three orders of magnitude from 0 μg/mL to 3000 μg/mL of ethyl-paraoxon, and the emission peak had a striking redshift, i.e., from 440 to 490 nm. However, unlike the sample in DI water (Fig. 6B), the F0/F had a good linear correlation to the concentration of ethyl-paraoxon in five-stage segments, i.e., 0–10, 10–100, 200–600, 600–1000, and 1000–3000 μg/mL. The fluorescent photos under UV 365 nm showed that the fluorescent brightness gradually decreased with the fluorescent color changes from bright blue to dark green (Fig. 7B inset). The linear relationships of F0/F vs. ethyl-paraoxon concentration (0–10, 10–100, 200–600, 600–1000, and 1000–3000 μg/mL) were separately shown in Fig. 7C–F. The linear equations were y = 0.058x+0.97 (n = 3, R2 = 0.97), y = 0.0078x+1.46 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), y = 0.026x-0.5 (n = 3, R2 = 0.97), y = 0.38x-212 (n = 3, R2 = 0.91) and y = 0.33x+155.64 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), respectively. The LOD was 0.06 μg/mL (by 3δ/slope), lower than in previous studies (Table S3).

In addition, the sensing performance of 0.05B-CDs@MgO in aqueous solutions between pH 4 to 10 was investigated. No apparent change in the fluorescence emission of pure B-CDs in aqueous solutions at pH 4–10 (Figs. S12A and S12B) was detected, consistent with the previous findings of our research team [67]. Moreover, the fluorescence performance of 0.05B-CDs@MgO was not sensitive to varied pH between 4 and 10 (Fig. S12C). It might be the protective effect of amino groups of B-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual and on-site detection of ethyl-paraoxon with a paper-based array sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated, the 0.05B-CDs@MgO is a promising candidate as a novel nanocomposite sensor for rapidly detecting and effectively degrading ethyl-paraoxon toxins. However, the conventional fluorescence spectrophotometer is always cumbersome and unsuitable for on-site detection.

Based on the above investigation, we first prepared a paper-based array sensor containing 0.05B-CDs@MgO, as shown in Fig. 8 A. Then, a portable chamber was assembled to determine the ethyl-paraoxon concentration via smartphones in a simpler way. It comprises a dark sample room, two ultraviolet lamps (365 nm), an observation area, and a smartphone shooting window. All designed accessories are shown in Fig. S13. Then, aliquot trace amounts (10 μL) of ethyl-paraoxon with the different concentrations in river water were added to the obtained paper-based array sensor containing 0.05B-CDs@MgO. After incubation for 30 min, it was placed in the chamber and observed and photographed under UV irradiation with a smartphone. Using the smartphone's color recognition app, we could calculate and convert the presented fluorescent colors into RGB values (Fig. 8B). The piecewise linear relationship between the green channel/blue channel (G/B) values and ethyl-paraoxon concentration (0–3000 μg/mL) were established, as shown in Fig. 8C. The detailed linear fitting results are shown in Fig. 8D–E. The G/B values had a good linear correlation with the concentration of ethyl-paraoxon in two ranges (0–100 and 100–3000 μg/mL) with a LOD of 3.48 μg/mL. And the linear equation were y = 0.0032x+0.58 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99) and y = 0.00013x+0.92 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), respectively. Fig. 8F compares results through the conventional fluorescent spectra and a paper-based array sensor in a portable chamber, as explored in this paper. The two strategies showed desirable results within 0–3000 μg/mL. Both strategies showed excellent linear relationships with ethyl-paraoxon concentration (R2 ≥ 0.91). The recovery rates of ethyl-paraoxon based on fluorescent sensing and paper-based sensor were 99–102% and 95–116%, respectively (Table S4). The relatively high recovery rate indicated that both strategies were reliable with good detection accuracy for quantitatively detecting ethyl-paraoxon. The error source of fluorescence spectrometry might be the interference of river background signals or other heteroions. The error of the paper-based array sensor was higher than the conventional fluorescence spectra method, which might be derived from measurement errors, the limitations of the smartphone camera resolution, interference from river background signals, and interference from other heteroions. Conventional fluorescent sensing was more reliable and could accurately distinguish the linear relationship in more segments within large concentration ranges with a lower LOD. However, the 0.05B-CDs@MgO nanocomposite and its paper-based array sensors could still be served as a promising candidate for detecting ethyl-paraoxon of trace amounts in actual water samples compared with previous studies (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro phytotoxicity and cytotoxicity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO
ce:section.ce:para: The potential ecotoxicity of nanomaterials has attracted a rising concern in the last few years. Thus, we conducted phytotoxicity and cytotoxicity assessments on 0.05B-CDs@MgO to investigate its environmental impact. The phytotoxicity was evaluated by estimating the germination and rhizome growth of the mung bean seeds after being treated with 0.05B-CDs@MgO for 1, 3, and 7 days (Fig. 9 A–C). All the seeds showed germination with 0.05B-CDs@MgO up to 500 μg/mL, while the shoot lengths decreased in a concentration-dependent manner, i.e., 10, 100, and 500 μg/mL. No significant changes were detected at a low dose of 10 μg/mL compared to negative controls.

The cytotoxicity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO on HSF cells was evaluated. HSF cells are host cells in human skin and dermis widely used for in vitro cytotoxicity studies. As shown in Fig. 9D, after treatment with 0.05B-CDs@MgO, the viability of HSF cells showed a significant concentration-dependent effect. At low concentrations of 1, 10, and 100 μg/mL, 0.05B-CDs@MgO showed no cytotoxicity to HSF cells compared with blank control. At 500 μg/mL and 1000 μg/mL of 0.05B-CDs@MgO, up to 90% and 49% of survival HSF cells could be detected, respectively. The cytotoxicity of 0.05B-CDs@MgO might be the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS).

In this work, the xB-CDs@MgO nanocomposites were facilely obtained. Then they were used to effectively on-site degrade and accurately detect ethyl-paraoxon. Among them, 0.05B-CDs@MgO showed excellent comprehensive performance due to the adequate ethyl-paraoxon degradation capacity of up to 50.89 mg/g and high fluorescence emission intensity between pH 4 and 10 with benign phytotoxicity and cytotoxicity. In actual river water, two strategies were developed based on 0.05B-CDs@MgO, i.e., fluorescent spectrometer and paper-based array sensor in a portable chamber. Both had desirable piecewise linear correlations to a wide range of ethyl-paraoxon (0–3000 μg/mL) with LOD of 0.06 μg/mL and 3.48 μg/mL, respectively. Moreover, the paper-based array sensor showed an excellent visual effect, which could be used to determine ethyl-paraoxon in actual river water. Especially it could be accomplished in a portable dark chamber to visually realize the quantitative detection of ethyl-paraoxon, which may provide new insight for developing a reliable, convenient, effective, and portable platform for paraoxon-related toxin on-site batch detection and detoxification.

Bo Liu: Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Meijie Cai: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Methodology. Xiangling Feng: Visualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Siyu Lu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Song Lin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Feng Tian: Methodology, Supervision.
